solution: 
```java
class TweetCounts {
    private Map<String, List<Integer>> tweetMap;

    public TweetCounts() {
        tweetMap = new HashMap<>();
    }
    
    public void recordTweet(String tweetName, int time) {
        tweetMap.putIfAbsent(tweetName, new ArrayList<>());
        List<Integer> times = tweetMap.get(tweetName);
        times.add(time);
    }
    
    public List<Integer> getTweetCountsPerFrequency(String freq, String tweetName, int startTime, int endTime) {
        if (!tweetMap.containsKey(tweetName)) {
            return new ArrayList<>();
        }

        List<Integer> times = tweetMap.get(tweetName);
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();

        int chunkSize;
        if (freq.equals("minute")) {
            chunkSize = 60;
        } else if (freq.equals("hour")) {
            chunkSize = 3600;
        } else { // "day"
            chunkSize = 86400;
        }

        int chunks = (endTime - startTime) / chunkSize + 1;
        for (int i = 0; i < chunks; i++) {
            result.add(0);
        }

        for (int time : times) {
            if (time < startTime || time > endTime) {
                continue;
            }
            int chunkIndex = (time - startTime) / chunkSize;
            result.set(chunkIndex, result.get(chunkIndex) + 1);
        }

        return result;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N + C), where N is the number of tweets for the given tweetName and C is the number of chunks in the time range
space complexity: O(T), where T is the total number of tweets stored across all tweetNames
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a HashMap to store tweets by name and a list of times. For getTweetCountsPerFrequency, it calculates the number of chunks and initializes a result list. It then iterates through the tweet times, counting how many fall into each chunk. The key strengths are efficient chunking and counting, while the main weakness is the potential for high memory usage if many tweets are stored.
